speaker: interviewer
text: This is the beginning of tape six with Morris Rubenstein. And what were you're just saying to me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have plans about where to go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Plans where to go? No, I didn't have no plans. I wanted to go to Israel in the beginning. I really wanted to go, but when the cousin sent the letter and she said, you know, we-- we-- we tried so hard and everything, you know, we're going to make up papers. You have to come to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did you come to? What year?

speaker: interviewer
text: To where?

speaker: interviewee
text: To-- I came to Boston. And from Boston I came-- we had to go to New York, but they changed, you know, from Amberg, I came to Boston. And in Boston, I had to wait on the ship because it was Washington's birthday. So-- from then--

speaker: interviewer
text: After, we-- I think the Joint distribution, you know, met us, met me. It was I and another man and a girl, you know? The man came to Detroit. I came to Detroit. The girl went to Flint. And what did you do when you came here?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came here, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a cousin--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: A living? First of all, worked in a laundry for two weeks, for a cousin. But I didn't like it. So-- because there was a guy there, he thought I will take away his job. So he told the cousin, you know, I don't do this good, I don't do this good.

speaker: interviewer
text: So and another cousin, he worked in a market, an Holbrook Market. So he asked me if I want to go to work in market. So I said, OK. So I went to work in a market, I worked. A supermarket?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fruit market, yeah. No, it was a grocery, fruit, everything, a big market-- like-- like Farmer Jack, you know? So I worked there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you meet your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: I met my wife in 1944?

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry, when did you meet your wife? 1954?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1954, Yeah, I met my wife 1954.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your wife's first and what was her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shapiro, Miriam Shapiro.

speaker: interviewer
text: And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] when did you begin to have children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, children in 1955.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many children do you have now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Four girls and three boys.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many grandchildren do you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think, 23, 23, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there a reason why you decided to have that many children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, because we were alone, you know? I didn't have nobody. No, I didn't have nobody. It was just me and my wife, you know, and I was alone since I was 13 years old, you know? I got married when I was 30, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was Miriam a survivor? Is Miriam a survivor?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think she is a survivor, but she came here before-- maybe a year before the war broke out with the Germans, I guess.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the two of you decided you would like to have a big family together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'll tell you, we didn't even decide, it just happened, you know? In-- in-- in the beginning, we didn't have anything. When we got married, we-- we came-- we came back. We went for our honeymoon to New York for a few days. And we came back, we didn't even have a place for to stay. So we stayed with my cousin for two weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after we find our apartment. And I worked in a-- when I came, I got laid off where I worked for the market. Then I was real, real-- a Jewish person, you know, I just got-- and I only had $50, you know, what, when I came, they gave me. And we didn't have where to stay, nothing, you know? And after, I found in another place, another market of work. And he played the horses. He-- he played-- he gambled, so he lost the market, so I got laid off. And he didn't pay us so after, we went to the-- what do you call it? You know what they collect the money, you know, if the employer don't pay you?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he told us it might take a little long, you know, you collect the money. So they collected the money for us. It was a few hundred dollars.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, eventually you started your own business though?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, And-- I went to work in another place, and he asked me how much I made a week. And after, he didn't even want to pay me, you know? I put in maybe 70, 80 hours for $30. So I decided to go in and-- into business. I went in-- I went into the bank. It's a long story--so I borrowed money.

speaker: interviewer
text: But tell me what your business is. What did you do? How did you support your family of seven?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I used to pick up scrap metal, you know? First I start picking up batteries and it was real hard, real hard, real hard. And then I start, in the factories, picking up shavings, turnings. You know what it is, turnings, shavings. Then I start picking up steel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what do you do with it? You dispose of it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I used to sell it to the scrap yards, you know, and I made a-- a profit, you know, just enough to support-- to support a family. And then the first landlord was all right, but the second landlord, he didn't give us no heat, nothing. You know, so we--

speaker: interviewer
text: You know what I want to ask you, which I think is-- which I think is interesting is, how the Holocaust affected how you have raised your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think that it did affect me. Real, real, real affect me, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: How did it affect how you brought up your children or how their lives were? How?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know what to say. I don't know. I really don't know what to say. I don't know how to put it. I know it affected me. I think it affected them, too. It did affect-- it did affect them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you talk with them about your experiences in the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not too much, not too much. I talked on the end with the younger one, with Anna and Sheila, too, you know. But the boys sometime they want to listen, and sometime they didn't want to listen, you know? But the main thing is my children, you know, [CRYING] and I don't know what to say. I just don't know. I don't know. I don't know. It did affect me.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did it affect you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It affect me in all my life after the war. And affect me in company, to my children, to my wife, and even to myself. My-- how I run the business, how I've worked.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, I used to dream, you know, the Germans were running after me, the Ukrainians, you know, in the ghetto. My father, my mother, and everything, you know? It did affect me. It did affect me and I don't how to put it-- how it did affect me, I don't know. Are you afraid? Are you scared?

speaker: interviewee
text: Afraid--I'm scared. I'm afraid. I'm afraid of people. I have-- I haven't got too much trust in people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like now I have to go to court because I parked the truck in the driveway, you know, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so I went-- I went to court. He asked me if I'm guilty or not guilty. So I tell him, I'm not guilty. What am I guilty for? So you're right on-- I have to come in January 30 to court. So I'm scared. I don't-- I'm scared for a policeman. If I see a uniform, I shake. You know, I'm scared for policemen or a uniform, any uniform. And when I come in company, you know, sometimes they look at me. Sometime, I don't know, hard to bring up conversation, you know, hard to talk to people even. Hard  to explain, hard to talk, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like even when I go to shul for [NON-ENGLISH]-- like now, [NON-ENGLISH], it is-- I have it Shabbos [NON-ENGLISH], on Saturday and Sunday. So sometimes they'll call me in, you know, to have [NON-ENGLISH]-- you know, I wouldn't go. A few times, a guy, you know, he said, I know you're ashamed. Come in, come in. He grabbed me. He took me in one time in the morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why are you ashamed?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are you ashamed of?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. You know, like they had like [NON-ENGLISH] and herring. So I took just a little piece. I didn't eat, you know? I don't know. I feel like it's given to me because I was begging bread. I was begging from the farmers and everything when-- when the Russians came in, I used to go into a restaurant and I just had a soup and a piece of bread. You know, and a lot of time I took two pieces of bread. I took to my mother. She didn't have nothing to eat. [SOBBING] So this would make me maybe like this. And I feel if I take it, it's like I'm begging it. You know, like, I went, you know, to ask for scrap metal. So I went out to a place, a young guy, a nice guy, so I asked him if he has metal. So I picked up some metal. I came in again.

speaker: interviewer
text: He said, Morris, what's the matter with you? You're a nice guy. You come in, it's like you're begging I should sell it to you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, don't be like this. He said, you know, ask if you-- you're paying for it. You're not taking it for nothing. You know, so finally, I went in, you know, and I started asking him, asking him for metal. And a lot of times I used to go into the office and the secretary used to say, we have somebody already. So then I used to go into the back and the foreman was there, or the purchase agent, or the boss himself. I asked him, so he said, here, come in. I'll show you what I have, you know, and I bought the metal. But she used to say-- but still, it's in me. It doesn't go away and this what it is going to be until I die. [CRYING] And I don't think the kids can understand me. I don't think they can understand me.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you could say one thing to your children so they could understand you, what would you say to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know what to say to them. I just don't know what to-- I love them with all my life. I don't know what to say to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't know why I'm crying, but I have to thank God I'm here, you know? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I really don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: See, if I would tell you the whole thing, it would-- it would take a day and a half. You know, but it's hard how I survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think you survived? Have you thought about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know how I survived. I just, like I was staying with the [NON-ENGLISH], and a farm lady invited us, all three of us, there was another Jewish guy to eat. And then I find out the Germans have to call me, called all [NON-ENGLISH], you know, to look for Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I go to the farmers, collect tax, you know? So the tax was, you know, they had to bring in the rye, sometimes cows, chickens, you know, to the Germans. So the guy opened the door from the-- from the little barn to go to the-- so he said, Moishe, Herschel, come. He grabbed me, Herschel. And he said, no, I'm not going to let you go. I'm not going to let you die.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he went, this other guy went. A tall guy, he was real educated. He went and the-- their guy, the [NON-ENGLISH], he talked Jewish, and he was an interpreter for the Germans. And when the Germans had to come to this village, he used to let us know. We used to run into the woods.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think it was luck?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was luck or it was from God. It was something, a miracle from God. Down in-- go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you a religious man?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not too religious. I was religious. I was, I used to light [NON-ENGLISH] every morning, every night. My father, mother were real religious. My father used to go like Shabbos morning for [NON-ENGLISH]. You know [nON-ENGLISH]? They used to go before and after, they used to come and have something, you know, we didn't cook but it was something what they kept warm, [NON-ENGLISH]-- You know, they used to put it--

speaker: interviewer
text: But you said your family was very religious.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very religious, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And today, you said what is--

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometime I daven, sometime I put [NON-ENGLISH] on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the Holocaust change your belief in God?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it changed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it changed. I don't know. I don't know, because for the three years I didn't daven, I didn't nothing. I ate pork-- what could you do? Like we were in a barn-- in a barn, and the farm lady brought out potatoes for the pigs and she put it in there. And we jumped down when she walked out, and we ate from the pigs, the potatoes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when I was on the farm, I was working, you know? They had pork. They had mashed potatoes. And then they fried the pork. They put in the mashed potatoes. The only thing I couldn't do is that I couldn't eat meat and milk. One time I had a little milk on the farm, you know, with the meat. It was terrible for me, you know? But when you came to this country, did you go to services and did you bring up a Jewish home?

speaker: interviewee
text: We have a Jewish home, you know, I mean, I don't eat [NON-ENGLISH], like anything. But we eat in a restaurant.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your children, are they are religious? Some?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three are real religious. And four, no, I think four are religious but three aren't religious.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me ask you why you decided to tell your story? Why this is an important thing for you to tell?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it's important to tell. I think all the world should know, you know? See, the Jewish people, they couldn't fight. They couldn't run away because everybody killed them. The Ukrainians killed them, some Polish killed them. Every country killed the Jews, not just the Germans. Half the Ukrainian, or some Polish, Romania, Lithuanians, you know, it would have been left a lot more, maybe a million or maybe a million and a half would have been left.

speaker: interviewer
text: A lot of them ran away but the Ukrainian killed them all. This was in our-- in the Ukraine, you know? Is there one message you would like to leave, as part of your story, in terms of why you wanted to tell your story-- what happened to you in the Holocaust? Is there a message you would like to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I hope they do, I hope they come out and tell the story. Some of them lived through worse than what I lived through, especially in the concentration camps. You know, and the real, real labor camps, you know? I know one, but he doesn't want to tell the story, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think about the Shoah, the Holocaust, often?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah, I often think about it. It's with me. It's with me. It's in me, you know? It just doesn't go away. It just doesn't go away. It's not going to go away. You know, if something like this-- I don't know how to explain. I don't know. I don't know how to-- how to say. Like in a place, you know, I was saved-- and the [NON-ENGLISH] saved me, too. We went-- we went into a village. And it was real, real cold. We couldn't stay outside. We went house to house to ask for food so he gave us, so they give us-- they give us onions. They said when you eat the onion, you'll warm up the blood-- they'll warm you up. Some give us bread. Then we went into one farmer and he told us there is a-- a barn. Nobody is occupying the barn. We can go in there to sleep. So we went into the barn and we went onto the straw.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in the morning, two Ukrainians, older ones and two young ones with forks. You know the long forks? And they started yelling Jude, get out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we didn't get out. They said, if you don't get out, we'll put the forks through you and they start. So we went out and they were standing with the forks. And he start yelling in Polish, you know, this boy. See, he was a refugee from Poland. You know, the parents, they run away from the Germans. So he talked good Polish and he said, he started yelling to them, don't kill Moishe, don't kill Moishe, don't kill Moishe. And while he was yelling, and I want to jump on it, but I would jump on the fork. And I had a knife here. And I just slid down, and I got the knife on one Ukrainian. And I forgot-- or I don't know, the blood start running and they start between them talking and arguing, and we run away. We run and then we start walking. What I want to tell you is what the mind-- and we didn't even look back if they're running after us or they're not run after us. And we went into the house and we asked for food and they gave us food, a lady. We finished the food and a big bread, both of us. And the lady crossed herself as we were eating, as we were finishing with food. And we walked out, we were just walking. And-- afraid-- we weren't afraid if the Ukrainian will run after us, what happen or nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we walked back, you know, to another farm, you know? This was before they killed the [NON-ENGLISH], you know? So it was a miracle too. It was so many miracles. And when I was staying with this [NON-ENGLISH], and-- and they were threatening me, should give me over to the Germans and everything. And they were just, the scale was weighing, you know, on it, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And how I came to this country and everything. I never dreamed about America. I never dreamed like to go to Israel. And I never even dreamed I would be alive, after-- I'll survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: I never dreamed the Russians will come back. I thought that's all. It's the end, you know? Is there anything else you would like to add or anything else you would like to say to anybody who might be watching this or to your family or to anyone about the Holocaust and what it's meant to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I hope it never happens again. You think it can happen? I hope it never, never happens. And another thing is, I'm glad our children are born in this country, you [CRYING] Because I-- I don't trust Poland, I don't trust Russia or Ukraine. I don't trust anybody. I don't even trust here. You know, I don't know what ever can happen here, just because we live like this, because I was afraid. If it wouldn't be for my wife, I wouldn't even buy the house. [CRYING] I didn't want to buy a house. You know, so she said, you know, I-- I want to live on this street but I want my, it should be my-- he didn't give us no heat, you know, another landlord.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you saying you are afraid to put down roots?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah I was afraid. I was real-- I was afraid I'm going to have to run again, you know? And I was afraid to move into a park even. They should make a ghetto, you know? [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Even now, we go to the Jewish Center to work sometime, you know? And I'm, real real afraid. I'm afraid to live among Jewish people because they're going to get them, [CRYING] you know? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This-- I'm still afraid. You know, like here, you can run, whatever, you know, but I hope it's not going to happen. Well, I'm old, I'm too old already for-- for this, everything. And sometimes I say, I don't care anymore, you know? But my life is the children. [CRYING] Well, you have a lot of those children and grandchildren.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have to thank God for it. I don't even know how we raised them, how we supported them, or everything. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.
